ProcScript and Memory

ProcScript and Memory

When a component is compiled, any ProcScript is compiled into a tokenized intermediate format. This intermediate format code is then interpreted by Uniface at run time. The Uniface runtime places the compiled ProcScript into memory when the component is loaded, and then interprets it, one statement at a time.

Note: No single block of compiled ProcScript may exceed 32 kilobytes. If the ProcScript in a trigger compiles to more than 32 kilobytes of object code, split the ProcScript into severalfunctionmodules.

Component variables are stored in the component variable table; the amount of memory required depends on the number of component variables. Global variables require a fixed amount of storage, which is not released until the application is exited. This is because global variables are shared between several different components and are valid for more than the duration of a single component.

Note: The maximum object size of a global ProcScript, including all its modules, is 2 gigabytes. The maximum size per individual module is 32 kilobytes.

Functions and Entries

Entries are defined as entry blocks and invoked using the call command.

Functions are defined as function blocks and invoked using the call command.

The tokenized ProcScript of a component is organized as one contiguous block in memory. This means that linear ProcScript (that is, ProcScript with no call statements) is executed without any overhead. However, a call statement causes Uniface to branch to the called code. The branch address is stored as an offset within the contiguous block.

Global ProcScript

Global ProcScript modules are loaded into memory as required. When a reference to a global ProcScript is first encountered by the ProcScript interpreter, the global ProcScript is loaded and stored in memory. The interpreter then binds this address to the call statement.

Because of the way global ProcScript modules are loaded on demand, the first time the global ProcScript is referenced by an application, there is an overhead while Uniface retrieves the global ProcScript and binds the address to that particular call statement. However, once the address of the global ProcScript has been bound, subsequent access to that global ProcScript from this particular call statement is much quicker.

If this global ProcScript is called by another call statement, the address of the global ProcScript is bound to this instance of the call statement. There is a delay while Uniface finds the location of the global ProcScript. To minimize the overhead of rebinding several different calls to the same global ProcScript, it can be advisable to define a local function routine to the global ProcScript and to access the global ProcScript only via this locally defined function.

Note: To improve performance, a global ProcScript is only loaded once. If you modify a global ProcScript during a Uniface application session, these modifications are not recognized until the application has been exited and restarted.

The ProcScript compiler issues a warning message if it cannot find a global ProcScript in the library specified for the component, the application shell or in SYSTEM_LIBRARY.

Global ProcScript and Global Variables

When compiling a global ProcScript that uses a global variable, the compiler checks for the variable in the following locations in the following Global Variable Libraries:

  1. SYSTEM_LIBRARY
  2. Library defined for the application
  3. Library defined for the component
  4. If the variable is not present in any of these libraries, the compiler issues a warning message.

At run time, Uniface optimizes references to global variables. For example, consider a global variable $$REG that is defined in LibProcA, LibProcB, and in SYSTEM_LIBRARY. Component A is defined to use LibProcA, while component B uses LibProcB; both components use $$REG. If one of the components alters the value of $$REG, this change will be noted in the other component, since both components use $$REG of the SYSTEM_LIBRARY.

Tokenized ProcScript Code Calling Local ProcScript Module Uniface stores a call address (L_STAR) as an offset within the contiguous block of memory containing the tokenized ProcScript. When the call is performed, processing is transferred to the entry point given as the offset. Global ProcScript Global ProcScript modules are loaded into memory as required. When a reference to a global ProcScript is first encountered by the ProcScript interpreter, the global ProcScript is loaded and stored in memory. The interpreter then binds this address to the call statement. Figure 2.
